sentences.text: Mhm.
Hey Christy.
Can you hear me?
Ok.
Yes, hi.
How are you doing today?
What's going on?
I'm not sure.
I
um thanks so much for taking the time to speak with me today.
I know it's a little late.
Uh but I appreciate, I appreciate you making yourself available.
Um I think we've had a lot of really good conversations with some of your, with some of your teammates at Embry.

So what I'm really looking forward to today is just kind of getting a little bit of a state of the union, uh How things are going, I'll share some updates that we have some insights I have about the usage patterns with IQ.
Um but would love to first just kind of understand uh what's been going on at Embry and what is on the docket for this year, kind of what your, your goals are and, and what the big initiatives are.
I've got a little bit of a,
a little bit of a slide show to prepare for you so we could start here um kind of uh aligning around this idea of success planning.

We're really looking to develop a framework so that I can go ahead every month, every quarter and just make sure that we're hitting the targets that you and your team initially wanted from joining IQ.
Um So I'll ask you a few questions there just so we can start that conversation and we'll kind of whittle that down as we work together.
Then we'll talk a little bit about the product usage where people are adopting IQ the most.
We'll jump into roadmap, talk about some exciting stuff we have coming.
Uh and then any next steps or deliverables, anything you want to add to this agenda before we get started or does that sound pretty good?
Ok.
All right.
So as far as the idea of success planning, um starting with what's going on at a really high level at embry kind of, what are your initiatives this year?
What are the key performance measures for you?
You know, how do people tell you that you're doing a good job and, and what are some of the challenges that you're seeing uh at the organization for 2024?
Um
So I don't know that we really have any apartment IQ related um initiatives.
Um No, anything uh company wide.
Yeah, let's go high level.
I want to understand kind of what the umbrella big picture is not at all.
I, I think our, our big, you know, push this year is we have um a bunch of em breed developed properties that are launching this year.
So, um, it's seven that are coming online and then additional third party management that we're taking over.
So, um, we'll have a minimum of 10, um, joining the, um, the management side of the company this year as far as new properties that we're taking on.
Um, so that'll be, um,
uh, roughly 25% growth.
We're, um,
yeah, so it's, you know, pretty significant, um, uh, left for us.
So, um, yeah, so it's gonna, you know, be a, you know, big, um, big change.
Um, so we're growing a lot in, um, Colorado, um, Scottsdale, um, Austin here in San Antonio.
So, um, and I think later in the year we'll have some in the Carolinas.
So that'll be a lot going on.
Um, as far as key performance measures, you know, their existing properties, um, you know, we're class a luxury and so it's, um, a challenge to keep those occupancies up in such a hard economy.

So, um, you know, just really, um, focusing on, you know, kind of that concierge level service to where we can, um, validate why somebody would, you know, want to, um, to push that envelope on paying for those rents and our properties over something that may be a little bit more affordable, um,

goals, I guess, you know, there again would be, um, you know, dealing with, with a lot of lips, um, trying to get them performing as best as they can and then the ones that are stabilized, um, you know, keeping those, those performance metrics up.
Um So, yeah, so it's quite a bit.
Yeah, that sounds like a lot.
And remind me Christy as far as we talked about the growth, sounds like we're going from the east coast all the way through Arizona.
Are you managing a lot of that?
Are you kind of overseeing that or is the team split up a bit?

Um So in each market we have um a regional manager that um you know, kind of heads with those teams and then of course, each property has their own staff of community managers, assistant leasing all of that.
Um So where my role comes in is just specifically on the marketing based initiatives.
So, um you know, getting them set up with the department IQ um all the different ilss, their website, all their branding, all their signage, um social media, all of that, got it.
Uh And then as far as doing the growth, uh do you, do you have a acquisitions department specifically or folks that handle that?
Um And I ask just because we're developing some products that would probably speak to those camps.
So as they come out, I'd love to have a few contacts ID to just kind of run them by.
Yeah, typically, like when you speak of acquisitions, you're talking about something that embry itself would be purchasing.
So we're not purchasing any of these.
So, um they're either embry built, um, or our development team is actually building from the ground up or we're just assuming the management of them but not the ownership of them.
Got it.
So we're not owners we build and we manage.
Um, sometimes we own, sometimes we a chore, but none of the ones that are coming in this year are a positions.
I see.
Ok.
That helps me understand that a little bit.
Uh, and then talk to me about, you know, measuring you, you talked about performance at the lease ops keeping the stabilized properties doing well, how do you evaluate that?
Is it just occupancy or are you looking to see things like days on market going down?
Are we looking at dollars per square foot?
Is that going up kind of how do we evaluate the health of these properties?
Um It's kind of just a balancing act.
So, I mean, it depends on how the property went into the year, you know, were they overpriced to begin with, um, or were they, you know, um healthy in that regard coming in?
So, um since we don't own all of them, um we have, um, you know, different goals based on, on who does own them.
So, um you know, some of our owners are very aggressive about pricing, others are more realistic.

Um So it just, it varies wildly across the portfolio um with the um new developments, um you're dealing with a property that was underwritten 34 years ago and so whatever they expected those rates to be at, um, again, you know, was that realistically done back then or was that based on, you know, pre COVID expectations?
So, um, it just, it just varies wildly.
So some of them, you know, they, they have very, you know, great underwriting and, and expectations going in and others are just
off the record.
Oh, man.
Yeah.
And then dealing with the changes in capital markets.
Now, I know I was just speaking to someone who they had everything basically underwritten like the month before the pandemic and it's just been an uphill battle for them nonstop.
Despite decent occupancy, they just can't get the numbers that they were expecting and the interest rates are forcing them to actually look to release the property.
Um despite it being such a positive performer for them.
So I know that can be tough.
Yeah.
And, um you know, I think a lot of it depends on, on perception, you know, if they think that their property is glamorous and gorgeous and everyone should want to pay those.
And in fact, it's a pig and no matter what and what owner doesn't want that.
Right?
And what owner doesn't see it that way.
Yeah.
So, I mean, it's, you know, it's everyone, every property has its own unique challenges.

Um Fortunately, if it's Emy Built, then we have a fantastic asset manager that has our back and, you know, works with us um to set more realistic goals of seen a month by month, but on the ones that we don't own, it's more challenging.
Got it.
Ok.
And of course, that varies wildly just by market too.
Some markets are over saturated with, with, you know, um,
product, so.
Mhm.
Ok.
I, I appreciate that.

And again, knowing kind of what's going on a level outside of apartment IQ kind of helps me just figure out what is, what's happening at embry and then hopefully find ways that IQ can kind of plug into these things.
So, like I mentioned, it's, it's absolutely, the teams are, are excited about it.
They're engaging with it.
Um, I think the more we can consistently, um, we have training periodically, um, it'll help them to, um, not necessarily that they need training, but just to make them sit down and go.
Oh, yeah, that's right.
I do have this tool.
Um, and maybe have, you know, some, um, hey, did you realize there's a school trick or, hey, you know, this, you know, this was a great case study that worked really well for this property.
Y'all might wanna consider it.
So, stuff like that, I think would help just keep them engaged.
Um, because right now they're excited and they're getting in there.
But I don't know, you know, once they get in the busy season, if they'll remember to take the time.
Um, and so I think that's, that's the key because I don't want them to go, you know, six months down the road.
Well, I never get in there and I never use it.
Well, but when you were using it, you loved it.
So how do we keep you getting in and seeing the value?
And because I mean, the first thing when I got a call saying, hey, you know, we had 10 leases and no, no bites.
My, this is my first go to because I, the recommendations tab and I'm like, ok, well, don't come complaining to me when you've got 50 proper, you know, 50 units that are all in red,
maybe you should have started there, you know, because I'll get reports every, uh, you know, usually daily, um, what the traffic is like.
And so if I see that they've had 15 tours for the week but no conversions, it's like, hm, that's a red flag.
Yeah.
And so I'm trying to encourage them to, like, you know, remember that they need to look here first, but I think it such a still such a new product for them and they're just not in that habit.
Um, you know, and, and I, and I wanna empower them
the lower people.
Um, you know, this is policing the community manager to bring that to the regional's attention.

You know, because the regional is really the one that would then bring that to the asset manager, but they're covering 567 properties so, you know, if, but not that many more like three or four, but still, I mean, if, if they don't think to look, I want somebody else to at least come to them and go, hey, you know, can we maybe check this out?
I noticed that this was going on, you know, and empowering all of them to kind of,
you know, raise that flag.

But again, I think it's getting them used to it and, and in that habit of going in there and looking and recognizing that this, you know, just because you looked on Monday doesn't mean that it's not gonna have changed by Friday.
Yeah.
Right.

I think that's, that's something that, as far as in the past, what I've done with other customers, um, having a bit more ownership a a and making them feel owner, uh, like they own this product, doing a lot of outreach to users who aren't logging in as often.
It helps to kind of set a baseline.
Right.
So you and I talking, hey, it'd be nice to see everybody logging in at least once per week based on the pricing meetings that they have and the cadence of other ownership calls.
We need to be in there at least once a week.
Uh, so I'll then look at the usage every quarter, every month and I'll say, hey, who are the users that aren't doing that?
And then I'll reach out to them directly and it starts real simple.
Hey, you're so, you know, we, we expect to see you logging in once a week so that you're informed for whatever the call is.
You can let me know what that call is and we'll say so you're informed for X meeting.
Uh, here's your login forgot password in case you forgot.
And then from there, we kind of keep tracking month over month.
The message changes a little bit based on how aggressive we wanna be or kind of the tack we wanna take.
Uh we do have some copy that we've developed 10 weekly tips and they go out in a stream right?
Once a week on Wednesday or Tuesday based on the kind of anticipated uh day that they have and that sort of thing, we've seen increase the adoption over time.
Uh because for a lot of folks, it's just, hey, I saw Apartment IQ looked really cool, but I had a million other things to do, right?
And I lost the website link.
I don't even know how to get there.

So there's that camp and then there's other issues that we can hope to resolve as we start engaging with folks and as they feel more comfortable with the tool, they can provide feedback that even helps inform our product team and helps inform our processes.
If I get a weird comment from somebody, I can bring it up to you and we can say, oh, let's work around that Right.
Maybe it's not working for them in this way.
Let's find a way for it to work.
So that's the kind of stuff that, that I like to do with my customers after we've had a couple of months of usage.
Um, so we can definitely start that going forward.
Yeah, I think that would be great.
Terrific.
Let me just make a note there.

Um,
yeah, I mean, I, I think that
it's gonna vary wildly across the whole portfolio.

We just need to drive people to the platform.
So as far as uh IQ experience overall thumbs up,
yeah, I mean, we've had a lot of people um you know, that held out that are now hearing everyone else talking about it that are also jumping on.
So, I mean, it, and, you know, we've had, um, you know, our asset manager, I, you know, sent him a thing and the next thing I know he's running his whole team on it.

So I'm gonna, I definitely feel like um there's been buy in, um I think it's just gonna take um, or for the teams that, that haven't had anything like this before or um, you know, a lot of them have like um the, the software that changed the prices for them.
So, um, you know, if they're used to relying on that, um then, then they're not really, you know, emotionally thinking, hey, this is the man you'd be looking at.

Um, you know, but I, I think that once they get in here and they start seeing, ok, this is what's really going on the market, you know, there's a zillion three bedrooms or a zillion two bedrooms or whatever it is, you know, I think that's going to make them start to go.
Oh, well, there's more to the story, you know.
And, right,
how do we get them to flex that muscle, that detective muscle?
Right.
And to start to go?
Ok.
Well, you know, how do I make my two bedroom stand out?

Sure.

Are there, are there maybe two or three regional managers perhaps, or even asset managers um that, you know, perhaps have really taken to the tool um that I might be able to reach out to kind of get their feedback and, and how they see their teams using it oftentimes when we do this, it kind of not, not quite a case study because it's nothing we'd publish.

Let's say we do a quarterly and brief focused training.

They just don't trip up.

Um for instance, PDF report exports if I ever get nervous.

I'll often check the PDF report exports because some folks prefer to get that weekly or even daily.

What you'll typically see is it starts high as people get the scheduling going and then it drops off because everybody who wanted it has it scheduled.
Whereas downloads can tend to stay kind of leveled out because this is kind of folks who needed it on the spot.
So in the last couple of months, it's definitely calmed down here in March.
But February and January for the number of users we have, this is pretty solid.
You want to be above like eight typically is what I see.
Uh And then as far as scheduled reports, I wanna see, at least half of the users have scheduled a report.
And so we have 15, 15.

So we're approaching 50 with some of these numbers and I know we have something like 60 users, I believe.
Uh So that's really good news for me just from like understanding how adopted and, and how kind of ingrained this product is getting with your team.

Another thing I like to highlight is the top report section because this tells us where in IQ are people spending most of their time, this big purple one, the property tab, most people overwhelmingly 68.11% of the time they're hanging out just in that property tab, they're probably hovering over the concessions.
They're understanding what the average pricing is at their property and kind of understanding where all the properties are fitting in.
I would love to see the units tab a little higher.
It's only 10% of all logins right now.

So definitely not seeing that as much.
I think in the next training, we do more training because if I don't think that they're digging into the understanding why and how to filter.
Right.
Exactly.

Um So that's what we'll focus on on the next training.
And then finally, I look at monthly, weekly and daily active users in this graph.
Here, we have the purple line representing our monthly active users and then that red orangish line doing weekly active users.
So I'm seeing that we tend to stick around 45 active users per week, which is a really solid percentage of the total user base, right?
If we know that we're about 60.
Um So most of our users are logging in at least once a month as we move down, 25 folks tend to log in per week.
Um Obviously, this changes where we are in the month.
Uh for instance, end of March, I guess we had a super big drop, but I would expect here at the start of the month for this to jump quite a bit since we're here at the beginning of April.
So the next time we talk, we'll be taking a look at this to see kind of how did this pattern play out as we came into April?
And did we get the spike or did we not?
And why not?
Are people not seeing this tool as useful anymore or what?
So this is kind of how I get informed and, and how I start drawing my conclusions too.
Any questions here about about some of this data or, or some of the conclusions we're drawing, feeling pretty good.
Cool.

Not sure if that's something I did actually speak with Amy Hood.

And so I wanna keep, you know, it's too early to kind of draw major conclusions yet.

I saw a wide variety of folks in the role report.

But let me know if there's anybody else you want to get introduced to IQ, I'd be happy to run them through a quick demo, quick training and get them access.
Ok,
as far as percentage of the portfolio in IQ um you mentioned 10 new properties coming in this year.

Let me know.

I have already fought that battle last year.
So, um you know, so far the seems to enjoy it terrific.
Let me know if that changes Ok.

Real quick.

and new metrics.
